stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the owner's conclusion?
passageOrStimulus: A bakery introduced a loyalty card that gives a customer a free pastry after ten purchases. In the three months since, the bakery's total pastry sales have risen by 25%. The owner concludes that the loyalty card caused the increase in sales.

choices.text: The bakery's main competitor closed its doors during the same three-month period.
Customers who enrolled in the loyalty card bought pastries far more frequently after enrolling than comparable customers who did not enroll.
The free pastry awarded after ten purchases is the least expensive item the bakery sells.
The bakery spent less on advertising this year than it did last year.
Some customers who signed up for the loyalty card never returned to the bakery.

distractorRationale.A: A competitor closing supplies an alternative cause for the sales rise, so it weakens rather than strengthens the owner's claim that the card was responsible. The correct strengthener instead shows the card's mechanism at work among enrollees.
distractorRationale.C: The price of the free pastry is irrelevant to whether the card caused the sales increase; it neither confirms nor rules out the causal link. To strengthen, you need evidence that enrollees actually bought more.
distractorRationale.D: Spending less on advertising does nothing to confirm the card drove sales—if anything it leaves the rise unexplained rather than tying it to the card. The right answer connects the increase directly to enrollees' behavior.
distractorRationale.E: That some sign-ups never returned points away from the card's effectiveness, weakening the conclusion rather than supporting it. The correct choice shows enrollees as a group bought more than comparable non-enrollees.

explanation: The owner makes a causal claim: the card drove the sales increase. (B) strengthens it by showing the proposed mechanism at work—enrollees bought more than comparable non-enrollees. (A) supplies an alternative cause (weakens); (C) and (D) are irrelevant to the cause of the rise; (E) weakens.

stem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion?
passageOrStimulus: A company switched to a four-day workweek and found that productivity, measured by tasks completed per week, rose by 15% over the next quarter. Management concluded that the shorter workweek caused the productivity gain.

choices.text: Employees reported being more satisfied with the four-day schedule than with the previous one.
Competing firms in the same industry did not adopt a four-day workweek.
The four-day workweek reduced the company's monthly office electricity costs.
During the same quarter, the company deployed new software that automated many of the routine tasks counted in the productivity measure.
Productivity at the company had been roughly flat for the two years before the change.

distractorRationale.A: Higher satisfaction is a pleasant side effect but says nothing about whether the schedule caused the task-count gain, so it does not weaken the causal claim. The weakener supplies a rival cause for the measured productivity.
distractorRationale.B: What competing firms did is irrelevant to whether the four-day week caused this company's gain; it offers no alternative explanation. The correct choice identifies new automation as the real driver.
distractorRationale.C: Lower electricity costs are an unrelated benefit of fewer office days and do not bear on the productivity measure. To weaken, you must explain the task-count rise some other way, as the automation choice does.
distractorRationale.E: Flat productivity before the change actually makes the post-change jump look caused by the new schedule, so it supports rather than weakens the conclusion. The weakener instead introduces the automation software as a confound.

explanation: The conclusion is causal. (D) weakens it by supplying an alternative cause: new automation software inflated the task count during the same period, so the shorter week may not be responsible. (A) and (C) are side effects; (B) is irrelevant; (E) tends to support the causal story rather than weaken it.

stem: It can be inferred that the Swiss and Japanese communities discussed in the passage

choices.text: depended on national governments to enforce resource limits
eventually sold their shared resources to private owners
sustained their resources through rules they devised themselves, rather than through privatization or state control
were ultimately unable to prevent overexploitation of their resources
raised penalties to the maximum from the very first violation

distractorRationale.A: This reverses the passage, which presents these communities as examples that were neither private nor state-run; relying on national governments contradicts their self-devised rules. The inference supported is that they governed themselves.
distractorRationale.B: Selling resources to private owners is the privatization route the passage explicitly contrasts these cases with, so it is the opposite of what can be inferred. They sustained the commons through their own rules.
distractorRationale.D: The passage cites these communities as successes, so concluding they failed to prevent overexploitation reverses the point. The supported inference is that their self-made rules kept the resources sustainable.
distractorRationale.E: This contradicts the passage's description of graduated sanctions that start mildly; penalties were not maxed out from the first violation. The correct inference concerns self-governance, not maximum penalties.

explanation: The passage offers these communities as cases of arrangements that were 'neither private nor state-run,' governed by rules the users themselves devised and monitored. That is (C). (A) and (B) contradict that; (D) reverses their documented success; (E) contradicts the description of graduated sanctions that begin mildly.

stem: According to the passage, one design principle shared by durable resource-managing communities was that those affected by the rules could

choices.text: take part in modifying the rules
transfer their access rights to outsiders
avoid penalties entirely for first offenses
appeal disputes to a central government agency
disregard the defined boundaries of the resource

distractorRationale.B: Transferring access rights to outsiders is not among the design principles described; the passage stresses clear boundaries, not open transfer. The stated principle is that affected members could help revise the rules.
distractorRationale.C: The passage describes graduated sanctions, which means first offenses still draw a (mild) penalty rather than none, so avoiding penalties entirely contradicts it. The correct principle is participation in revising rules.
distractorRationale.D: Appealing to a central government agency contradicts the passage's emphasis on self-governance rather than external authority. The principle named is that those bound by the rules could participate in changing them.
distractorRationale.E: Disregarding boundaries runs against the passage's point that clearly defined boundaries were a key design principle. The shared principle the question asks about is participation in revising the rules.

explanation: Among the design principles, the passage states that successful groups 'ensured that those affected by the rules could participate in revising them'—(A). The other options contradict the passage's emphasis on clear boundaries, graduated sanctions, and self-governance rather than appeal to a central authority.
